mechanism: Structured memory encoding and retrieval with independent feature decay over time delays. Features of objects are stored in distributed representations where decay of feature-specific information occurs independently rather than as bound units. Observed via conditional probability analysis controlling for guessing and relative memory performance across delays.

Dyadic exchange model with asymmetric reciprocation. Positive behavior (generosity) maps to subsequent equality; negative behavior (greed) propagates with amplification; neutral (equality) reproduces in kind. Effect driven by affect valence rather than endowment, mediated by negative affect for greed propagation.

Multifactor account of memory encoding: retrieval practice enhances cue-target relational and item-specific processing but suppresses intertarget relational processing. Whether effect is positive or negative depends on task requirements: cued recall (uses cue-target relations) shows positive testing effect; free recall (uses intertarget relations) can show negative effect when intertarget structure is salient.

Information integration during judgment under cognitive constraints. Unconscious thought allows greater processing capacity and bottom-up integration of multiple cues; avoids top-down stereotyping and inadequate cue weighting that plague conscious deliberation. When complexity requires integrating many weighted cues simultaneously, unconscious processing outperforms conscious.

Motor simulation of articulation kinematics induces affect via embodied metaphor. Consonantal stricture points wandering front-to-rear during articulation simulate deglutition (swallowing), activating approach-related positive affect. Rear-to-front wandering simulates expectoration (spitting), activating avoidance-related negative affect. Effect is absent without subvocal pronunciation simulation.

Attribution of responsibility modulates perceived moral worth of actions. When actor is motivated by communal obligation system rather than individual choice, observers infer helping is side effect of satisfying obligation, not primary goal. This reduces attributed responsibility and intentionality, which fully mediates reduction in perceived morality.
